plain_0: I'm going to tell you the worst due-diligence story I know. It's mine.
plain_1: If you're about to buy a SaaS business, read this before you wire a single dollar.
plain_2: Two years ago, I found a SaaS business listed at $1.1M. $42K MRR. Three years of growth.
plain_3: The CIM showed a clean 2.3% monthly churn rate. The seller was transparent — or so I thought.
plain_4: He handed over a ProfitBird summary, cohort charts, and a churn dashboard screenshot. Everything
plain_5: looked healthy. My due diligence checklist had 40 boxes. I checked all 40. The deal closed.
plain_6: I was a careful buyer. I'd read every SaaS acquisition guide. I knew the right questions to ask.
plain_7: I asked them. I got good answers. I trusted the numbers because the numbers were presented by
plain_8: someone who understood how to present numbers.
plain_9: Four months post-close, MRR started declining. Not crashing — bleeding. Slow, steady,
plain_10: the kind of decline you don't notice until it's too late. I pulled the subscription data and
plain_11: ran the churn calculation myself for the first time.
plain_12: The real monthly churn wasn't 2.3%. It was 9.4%. Over four times what the CIM reported. That gap — the difference between the number I trusted and the number that was real — was worth $340,000.
plain_13: How did the seller get 2.3% when the real number was 9.4%? He hadn't lied. He'd used every
plain_14: legitimate-looking methodology trick available: he excluded downgrades from churn (revenue
plain_15: disappeared but the logo stayed). He reported the best cohort month as "representative." He
plain_16: excluded involuntary cancellations (failed payments). He counted reactivations as continuous
plain_17: subscriptions. Every single one of these choices is defensible in isolation. Combined, they
text_19: I hadn't asked for the raw CSV. I'd asked for a churn number. I got the number the
plain_20: seller wanted me to have. And I paid for it.
plain_21: After that deal, I did something obsessive. I pulled the raw subscription CSV from every SaaS
plain_22: acquisition opportunity I evaluated — not the summary, not the dashboard, the raw export
plain_23: with customer ID, MRR, plan, status, dates. I built a spreadsheet to compute churn five different
plain_24: ways: logo churn, revenue churn, net churn, gross churn, and cohort-adjusted churn. I cross-referenced
plain_25: concentration risk. I flagged inactive paying accounts.
plain_26: The only number that matters is the one you compute yourself from the raw data.
plain_27: If you're trusting a summary number someone else computed, you're not doing due diligence —
plain_28: I ran this analysis on 14 more deals over the next year. In 11 of them, real churn was meaningfully
plain_29: higher than reported. In 3, it was the same. Zero times was real churn lower. The gap averaged 4.2x.
plain_30: Every deal where I caught the gap, I either renegotiated the price or walked. Every deal saved me
plain_31: more than the cost of the analysis by a factor of 100.
plain_32: The spreadsheet worked, but it took me 4 hours per deal. And I kept making the same analysis
plain_33: manual mistakes. So I automated it. Then I showed it to two other acquirers I knew. They asked
plain_34: to use it. Then their friends asked. That's when I realized this wasn't a spreadsheet —
plain_35: it was the thing I wished I'd had before I wired $1.1M for a business worth $760K.
plain_36: Churn Lens is that spreadsheet, systematized. Upload the seller's raw subscription CSV. Get
plain_37: every churn metric computed five ways. See concentration risk, annual-plan churn exposure,
plain_38: inactive accounts, and a composite Revenue Quality Score — in minutes, not hours.
plain_39: Right now, 80% of SaaS acquisitions overpay because buyers trust seller-computed churn.
plain_40: The information asymmetry in SaaS M&A favors the seller at every step — they know
plain_41: the data, they compute the metrics, they present the summary. Churn Lens exists to flip that
plain_42: asymmetry. When every buyer demands the raw CSV and runs their own analysis, the era of
plain_43: hidden churn ends. That's the world we're building toward.
plain_44: If you're evaluating a SaaS acquisition right now, you probably hold at least one of these beliefs.
plain_45: I held all three. They cost me $340K.
text_46: FALSE BELIEF #1 (Vehicle): "The seller's churn methodology is industry-standard, so the number is reliable."Reality: There is no standard. Every seller makes methodology choices that flatter their number. The only reliable number is the one you compute from raw data.
text_47: FALSE BELIEF #2 (Internal): "I'm not technical enough to analyze raw subscription data myself."Reality: You don't need to be. You need the CSV and a tool that computes churn five ways. The analysis takes minutes, not a finance degree.
text_48: FALSE BELIEF #3 (External): "The seller wouldn't hide churn from me — they'd get caught in the earnout."Reality: Most churn methodology choices are legal, defensible, and invisible. Earnouts don't protect you from legitimate-looking accounting that understates real churn by 4x.
plain_49: Already have a target in due diligence?
